title: Diffusion for the (Markov) Anderson model
speaker: Jeffrey Schenker
affiliation: Michigan State University; Member, Institute for Advanced Study
abstract: I will discuss the proof by Yang Kang and myself of diffusion for the Markov Anderson model, in which the potential is allowed to fluctuate in time as a Markov process. However, I want to highlight the method of the proof more than the result itself and to speculate a bit about how things might work for the Anderson model itself.

proof_stages.label: Quantum-diffusion target for the static Anderson model
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the desired diffusive scaling and central-limit behavior for the Anderson Schrödinger evolution, explains the energy-resolved diffusion matrix, and reviews why the fixed-disorder problem remains out of reach despite localization and weak-disorder results.

proof_stages.label: Augmented-space lifting and joint translations
proof_stages.summary: A random matrix element of a function of the Hamiltonian is rewritten as a deterministic inner product on L2(Z^d x Omega), where joint shifts of the particle and environment commute with the lifted Hamiltonian and can be Fourier diagonalized into fiber operators h_hat(k) on disorder space.

proof_stages.label: Spectral interpretation of the Hamiltonian fibers
proof_stages.summary: The fiber operators are analyzed heuristically and through a prior result: at lambda=0 the constant function gives an embedded eigenvalue, turning on disorder should create a resonance, and perturbation plus Wegner estimates yield uniform Hölder bounds on the integrated density of states.

proof_stages.label: Density-matrix Liouvillian fibers for probabilities
proof_stages.summary: To access expected squared amplitudes, the wave evolution is replaced by density-matrix evolution under the Liouvillian commutator, lifted to an augmented space with two spatial variables and disorder, and Fourierized to fiber generators L_hat(k) acting on one spatial difference variable and the environment.

proof_stages.label: Formal perturbation near k=0 and the diffusion matrix
proof_stages.summary: For the static Anderson Liouvillian, the zero fiber has a zero mode, the first k-derivative is orthogonal to it, and the formal second-order perturbation produces a diffusion matrix involving the inverse of L_hat(0) on the orthogonal complement, explaining k~t^{-1/2} but leaving an unjustified spectral inverse.

proof_stages.label: Markovian disorder as a coercive regularization
proof_stages.summary: The model is changed to a time-dependent Markov potential, which adds the Markov generator B to each Liouvillian fiber; assuming the static disorder law is invariant and B has a spectral gap on mean-zero functions supplies positive real part away from constants.

proof_stages.label: Block/Feshbach control of the zero fiber
proof_stages.summary: The operator L_hat(0)+B is decomposed into disorder-constant and mean-zero blocks, and the potential coupling W, built from omega(x)-omega(0), couples all constant-sector directions except the conserved vector, giving a bounded inverse on the orthogonal complement and legitimate perturbation theory for an isolated zero eigenvalue.

proof_stages.label: Diffusion theorem for the Markov Anderson model
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the resulting theorem that diffusion holds for the Markov Anderson model under the gap and nondegeneracy assumptions, then discusses independence requirements, concrete generators, and the formal relation of the diffusion coefficient to small-disorder and diagrammatic calculations.

examples.name: One-dimensional localization under diffusive scaling
examples.note: The one-dimensional Anderson model is cited as a regime where the diffusive-scale diffusion constant is zero because the wave remains localized, illustrating why the scaling statement can miss the true dynamics.
examples.engagement_reason: he states that in dimension one the Anderson model is localized and gives zero diffusion constant on diffusive scales, but does not compute or analyze the 1D case further

passes.reason: The one-dimensional Anderson case is the lower-dimensional localized regime whose diffusive scaling collapses to diffusion constant zero, used as a caveat to genuine diffusion.
The one-dimensional Anderson case is the lower-dimensional localized boundary case with collapsed zero diffusion, used to show diffusive scaling can miss true propagation.
the one-dimensional Anderson case gives a collapsed diffusive limit with diffusion constant zero and is used to show that diffusive scaling can miss localization.
The one-dimensional Anderson case is a low-dimensional boundary case whose diffusive scaling collapses to zero diffusion, and it is used as an enemy case to genuine nontrivial diffusion.

the one-dimensional localized regime gives collapsed zero diffusivity at the minimal dimension and is used to show naïve diffusive scaling can mask non-propagation
The one-dimensional Anderson case is invoked as a failure of genuine diffusion, with the diffusive scaling collapsing to a zero diffusion constant.
The one-dimensional Anderson case is used as a localized zero-diffusion obstruction to genuine quantum diffusion and is a lowest-dimensional/zero-diffusion boundary case.
One-dimensional Anderson localization is used as the low-dimensional obstruction where diffusive scaling collapses to zero diffusion rather than true propagation.

examples.name: Weak-disorder kinetic/diffusive limit
examples.note: The Erdős–Yau–Salmhofer result is used as a comparison case where diffusion is proved only in a small-disorder limit with kinetic and diffusive rescaling, not at fixed disorder.
examples.engagement_reason: he describes the known weak-disorder kinetic/diffusive scaling limit and cites the Erdős–Yau–Salmhofer result without deriving it or running estimates in that regime

passes.reason: The weak-disorder result is a boundary perturbative limit as λ→0, where the random perturbation tends toward the free model, and is cited as a positive partial theorem.
The weak-disorder limit λ→0 is a near-free boundary scaling regime, presented as a positive partial diffusion result rather than a refutation.
the weak-disorder result is an asymptotic boundary regime as the disorder strength tends to zero, not an objection to diffusion.
The weak-disorder kinetic/diffusive result is a boundary scaling regime as λ→0, presented as a positive known limit rather than a refutation.
the weak-disorder result is a boundary asymptotic regime as the disorder strength tends to zero, cited as a positive known limit theorem
the weak-disorder result is a boundary scaling limit as λ→0 where the random perturbation vanishes, cited as a positive theorem rather than an obstruction
The weak-disorder result is a positive limiting theorem at the boundary λ→0 rather than an adversarial example.
The weak-disorder result is cited as a positive partial theorem in the singular boundary regime λ→0 rather than as a failure case.
The Erdős–Yau–Salmhofer weak-disorder result is a positive boundary-limit regime with the disorder strength tending to zero.
the Erdős–Yau–Salmhofer weak-disorder result is cited as a positive known scaling limit at the boundary λ→0
Cited as a positive theorem in the λ→0 boundary regime where the disorder asymptotically vanishes
the weak-disorder λ→0 regime is a boundary limit where the random perturbation vanishes, cited as a positive kinetic/diffusive theorem

examples.name: Brownian rotations of the potential variables
examples.note: In response to a question about the Markov generator, the speaker gives the model omega_t(x)=cos(theta_x+B_t^x), whose generator is formally an infinite-dimensional Laplacian and serves to make the abstract generator B concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: he writes a concrete Brownian-rotation model with �omega_x=�cos	heta_x, evolves the angles by Brownian motions, and identifies the corresponding Markov generator as a formal infinite-dimensional Laplacian

examples.name: Poisson spin-flip environment
examples.note: A spin-flip dynamics with jumps at Poisson times is mentioned as a convenient Markov environment whose generator has explicit spectral levels, illustrating how the formal diffusion-constant calculation becomes computable.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the generator/perturbation discussion to Poisson spin flips, using its point spectrum levels around 1/T and inserting that structure into the diffusion-constant/Feshbach expression

passes.reason: The Poisson spin-flip model uses minimal two-state local variables with a simple pure-point generator, introduced as a computable Markov environment rather than a refutation.
Equal-rate Poisson spin flips form a very regular Z2-symmetric product environment with computable point spectrum, not a counterexample.
